turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I’m studying natural environment, but I’m not sure it’s the right fit.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Thanks for sharing that. What parts of the course do you enjoy, and which parts feel difficult or uninteresting?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I enjoy fieldwork, but I struggle with heavy data analysis.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That makes sense. Some careers combine environmental work with more practical or people-focused roles.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: That sounds like something I’d enjoy.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: For example, environmental consulting, sustainability coordination, or policy support roles focus less on raw data and more on application.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: So what are some signs that a course or subject might not be the right fit for me?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Common signs include constant lack of motivation, stress that doesn’t improve, or difficulty seeing how the subject connects to your future goals.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I do feel unsure about job options.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: That’s very common. Talking to graduates or doing short internships can really help clarify that.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: What should I do now if I want to apply for internships next year?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Start by updating your CV, identifying 3–5 organisations you like, and building relevant skills through volunteering or short courses.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: I see. Do you think I need more work experience before applying?

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: Not necessarily—many internships expect beginners. Showing interest, basic skills, and motivation is often enough.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.text: That makes me feel more confident.

turns.speaker: bot
turns.text: I’m glad to hear that. We can review your CV together if you’d like.
